statement: CrewAI models every agent as a named role with a goal and a backstory, not as a generic callable. The backstory is injected into the system prompt and shapes how the LLM frames every response. Skipping or genericizing the backstory degrades output quality because the LLM loses the persona context that makes role-specific reasoning coherent.

CrewAI tasks are first-class objects that carry a description, expected_output, and an assigned agent. The Crew orchestrator passes the previous task's output as context to the next task automatically in sequential mode. This means task descriptions must be written as standalone instructions, not as conversational follow-ups, because each task description is re-rendered into a fresh prompt for its assigned agent.

CrewAI agents can enter infinite tool-call loops when a tool returns an error message that the LLM interprets as a signal to retry. The agent re-invokes the same tool with the same arguments indefinitely because there is no built-in retry-limit guard at the tool level. The fix is to set max_iter on the agent (default 25) and to make tool error messages explicitly instructional ('Do not retry; report this error to the user') rather than diagnostic.

When a task output is very long and is passed as context to the next task, CrewAI does not automatically truncate or summarize it before injection. If the combined prompt exceeds the LLM's context window, the provider silently truncates from the left, dropping the original task instructions and leaving only trailing output. This causes the downstream agent to act on incomplete information with no error raised. Mitigation: set output_file on large tasks and have the downstream agent read the file via a tool rather than relying on inline context passing.

CrewAI supports two process modes: sequential (tasks run in list order, each receiving the prior output) and hierarchical (a manager agent dynamically assigns tasks to worker agents using a sub-task planning loop). Hierarchical mode uses additional LLM calls for planning and is significantly more expensive and slower. Sequential mode is the right default for deterministic pipelines; hierarchical mode is only warranted when the task decomposition itself cannot be known at design time.

CrewAI tools are subclasses of BaseTool with a name, description, and _run method. The description is what the LLM reads to decide whether to invoke the tool — it must be written as a decision rule ('Use this tool when you need X'), not as an implementation note. Tools are attached per-agent, not per-task, so an agent's full tool list is present in its prompt on every task, which inflates token usage when an agent has more than 4-5 tools.

CrewAI uses LiteLLM under the hood for LLM calls, but agent-level LLM configuration accepts either a string model identifier or an instantiated LLM object (e.g., ChatOpenAI, ChatAnthropic). When mixing providers across agents in the same crew, the instantiated-object form is required because the string form assumes a single global provider config. Passing 'anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022' as a string to one agent while another agent holds a ChatOpenAI instance will silently share whatever API keys LiteLLM resolves from the environment, causing cross-provider key conflicts on teams with scoped credentials.
